front: What is the core principle behind a Stack data structure?
back: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out). The last element added to the stack is the first one to be removed, similar to a stack of plates.

front: What is the primary disadvantage of implementing a stack using a fixed-size array?
back: It has a limited, fixed capacity. If more elements are pushed onto the stack than the array can hold, it results in a stack overflow error.

front: What is the function of the 'top' pointer (or index) in an array-based stack?
back: The 'top' index keeps track of the position of the last inserted element (the top of the stack). It is used to push new elements and pop existing ones.

front: What are the two essential operations for adding and removing elements from a stack, and what are their common names?
back: Push (to add an element to the top of the stack) and Pop (to remove and return the top element from the stack).

front: What are the time complexities for the Push and Pop operations in a stack implemented with an array, and why?
back: O(1) constant time for both. This is because these operations only involve accessing or updating a single element using the 'top' index, with no need to shift other elements.
